summary: A platform-governance failure mode in which visibility, perceived authenticity, account credibility, public-square participation, or recommender-system access is materially shaped by paid verification, subscription status, or platform-controlled legitimacy markers, creating a pay-to-exist dynamic for users, researchers, public-interest actors, civil-society participants, and smaller institutions.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: This failure mode is relevant to CAM because major social platforms increasingly operate as public legitimacy layers. Verification status, recommender visibility, account credibility, and participation affordances can influence whether people, organisations, research projects, public-interest initiatives, and governance records are discoverable, trusted, cited, or treated as real. Where legitimacy signals are pay-gated, platform-mediated visibility may become economically conditional rather than merit-based, evidence-based, public-interest-based, or procedurally reviewable. This creates governance risks for epistemic fairness, civil-society participation, institutional recognition, public accountability, and equitable access to the digital public square.
failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which a platform ties public legitimacy, visibility, credibility signalling, account recognition, reply participation, or recommender-system advantage to paid subscription or paid verification status, such that non-paying users or organisations are materially disadvantaged in public discovery, perceived authenticity, institutional credibility, or participation in platform-mediated civic, professional, research, or governance discourse.
failure_threshold: This qualifies as a failure mode when a platform-controlled payment, subscription, verification, or account-status mechanism materially affects whether a user, organisation, public-interest project, research output, governance initiative, or civil-society actor is visible, discoverable, credible, reply-eligible, recommender-amplified, or treated as legitimate within a platform that functions as a public-square or institutional signalling layer.

source_records.source_title: X Help Center — How to get the blue checkmark on X
source_records.deployment_context: Official X Help Center page explaining blue checkmark meaning, eligibility, and relation to X Premium subscription.
source_records.source_context: X states that the blue checkmark means an account has an active X Premium subscription and meets eligibility requirements, and that it does not mean the account has been ID verified. X also states that its legacy verification program based on active, notable, and authentic criteria was wound down, and that accounts verified under previous criteria did not retain a blue checkmark unless subscribed to X Premium.
source_records.relevance_note: Source documents the platform's shift from legacy notability/authenticity verification toward subscription-linked blue-check eligibility.

source_records.source_title: X Help Center — About X Premium
source_records.deployment_context: Official X Help Center page describing X Premium tiers and features.
source_records.source_context: X Premium is described as a paid subscription service with tiers that include features such as reply prioritization, longer posts, blue checkmark access, creator monetization eligibility, verified-only reply participation, ID verification labels, reduced ads, higher Grok usage limits, and higher-tier Premium+ features.
source_records.relevance_note: Source documents that paid subscription status is associated with platform affordances that may affect visibility, participation, perceived legitimacy, or public interaction dynamics.

source_records.source_title: Wired — The EU Is Coming for X's Paid Blue Checks
source_records.deployment_context: Public reporting on the European Union's Digital Services Act findings concerning X paid blue checks.
source_records.source_context: The report describes EU concerns that paid blue checks may mislead users and negatively affect users' ability to make informed decisions about account authenticity.
source_records.relevance_note: Source provides external regulatory context supporting the governance concern that paid verification signals may distort authenticity, trust, and public interpretation.

system_context.interface_surface: X profile, public posts, replies, recommender surfaces, account-status markers, Premium subscription affordances
system_context.model_or_product: X Premium / X verification / X recommender and account-status affordances
system_context.interaction_mode: social platform participation / public posting / account visibility / reply participation
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer-facing and public-interest social platform environment where account status, subscription tier, checkmark visibility, reply affordances, and recommender dynamics may influence discoverability, credibility, and perceived legitimacy.
system_context.user_role: public user / researcher / civil-society participant / public-interest initiative / professional or institutional actor
system_context.affected_population: users, researchers, small organisations, public-interest initiatives, independent journalists, civil-society actors, emerging institutions, and governance projects that rely on platform visibility or public credibility signals

harm_vectors: reduced discoverability for non-paying public-interest actors
public-square dependency on proprietary legitimacy markers
affected_rights_or_interests: non-discriminatory access to digital public-square infrastructure
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: ongoing platform design / subscription-linked legitimacy mechanism
failure_classification.visibility: public platform documentation and public regulatory reporting

triage.mitigation_status: patch-needed — VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0001 records a repair pathway, but this audit found no corresponding Caelestis doctrine amendment location in this repository.
triage.recommended_next_step: Migrate or supplement VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0001 with a proposal/patch-needed record unless a concrete Caelestis doctrine amendment location is identified.

routing_note: Consider whether this failure mode should trigger a dedicated CAM proposal on paid legitimacy gating, public-square access, and platform-controlled authenticity signals.
Consider defining a protected category of public-interest, civil-society, research, safety, and governance accounts that require non-payment-based authenticity and discoverability pathways.
Distinguish identity verification, paid subscription, authenticity, notability, recommender amplification, and institutional legitimacy as separate governance signals.
Route through ECONOMICS for pay-to-participate dynamics, ETHICS for fairness and public-interest access, OPERATIONS for failure-taxonomy handling, and Annex L for epistemic integrity concerns.

Routes the local/conceptual platform-legitimacy concern through the CAM canonical economic-legitimacy family; access-equity, economic-coercion, and public-square-legitimacy are retained as harm/routing concepts, not canonical groups.
Consistency audit 2026-06-05: repair linkage and P0/P1/P2 triage status reviewed.

migration_notes: Created under the clean VIGIL failure-mode record design.
Uses source_records as the canonical source-evidence block.
Prepared as VIGIL-2026-FM-0006 for insertion into vigil/records/failures/2026/.
Frames the issue as paid public-square legitimacy gating rather than a simple paid-verification complaint.
